title: Kung Pao Chicken

ingredients.name: boneless chicken (breast or thigh), cut into 2 cm cubes

subs.name: 500 g paneer or firm tofu, cut into 2 cm cubes
subs.notes: fry the paneer/tofu first until golden before returning to the sauce

subs.name: cashews, dry-roasted
subs.notes: add at the same stage; cashews are softer than peanuts

ingredients.name: dried red chillies, halved and deseeded
visual_checks: adjust to heat tolerance; deseeding reduces fire while keeping flavour
ingredients.notes: adjust to heat tolerance; deseeding reduces fire while keeping flavour

subs.name: 4 whole dried Kashmiri chillies or guntur chillies
subs.notes: widely available in Indian groceries; the Kashmiri variety gives colour without excessive heat

ingredients.name: spring onions, cut into 2 cm pieces
cornflour
visual_checks: 1 tsp for marinade, 1 tsp for sauce slurry
ingredients.notes: 1 tsp for marinade, 1 tsp for sauce slurry

ingredients.name: Sichuan peppercorns or black pepper, ground

steps.text: Marinate the diced chicken with 1 teaspoon soy sauce, 1 teaspoon cornflour, a pinch of salt and ¼ teaspoon ground pepper. Mix well and leave for 15 minutes.
Mix the sauce in a small bowl: remaining soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, the second teaspoon of cornflour and 2 tablespoons of water. Stir until the sugar and cornflour dissolve and set aside.

Heat 2 tablespoons of oil in a wok or kadai over high heat until shimmering. Add the dried chillies and stir for 20 seconds until they darken and become fragrant — watch carefully as they burn quickly.

Add garlic, ginger and the remaining ground pepper. Stir for 30 seconds until fragrant, then add the marinated chicken in a single layer. Leave undisturbed for 1 minute to sear, then toss and cook for 2–3 more minutes until cooked through.
Push the chicken to the sides of the wok. Add the remaining oil in the centre, then add the spring onions. Stir-fry for 30 seconds.
Give the sauce a quick stir and pour it over everything. Toss vigorously for 1 minute until the sauce thickens and glazes the chicken.
Remove from heat and fold in the roasted peanuts — adding them at the end keeps them crunchy. Taste and adjust salt. Serve with steamed rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: peanut → cashews, dry-roasted (add at the same stage; cashews are softer than peanuts); chicken → 500 g paneer or firm tofu, cut into 2 cm cubes (fry the paneer/tofu first until golden before returning to the sauce); vinegar → 1 tbsp lemon juice (adds brightness but less complexity); dried-red-chilli → 4 whole dried Kashmiri chillies or guntur chillies (widely available in Indian groceries; the Kashmiri variety gives colour without excessive heat)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Verkadalai (roasted groundnuts) are your peanuts — remove the skin. Kashmiri or guntur dried red chillies are perfect substitutes for Chinese dried chillies. Cook in a kadai on the highest gas setting you have; the sear on the chicken makes a big difference. Soy sauce and vinegar are available at any Indian supermarket. Sichuan peppercorns can occasionally be found in metro spice markets; black pepper plus a pinch of ajwain (omam) gives a vaguely similar warmth.
